questions.question: Select all that are true about the three beats of the backtracking template.
choices: Choose appends the current element onto the shared path.
Explore recurses one level deeper with that choice in place.
Un-choose pops the element back off so the path is restored before the next branch.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Choose pushes onto the shared path, explore recurses, and un-choose pops to restore the path, so all three descriptions hold.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why you must record a copy of the path when you save an answer.
choices: The path is one shared list that later pushes and pops keep mutating, so a stored reference would change.
Recording a snapshot with path[:] freezes contents that later mutations cannot touch.
Copying is needed only so the saved answers sort correctly.
Without copying, Python refuses to run the recursion at all.
None of the above
questions.explanation: You copy because the live path is mutated by later pushes and pops, so a snapshot keeps each saved answer independent. It has nothing to do with sorting or a syntax requirement.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the start index in the subsets walk.
choices: Looking only forward makes each subset appear exactly once, built in increasing position order.
It turns the depth-first walk into a breadth-first one.
It caps every subset at two elements.
It sorts the input array as a side effect.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The start index only looks forward, so each subset is built once in increasing order. It does not change the traversal order, cap subset size, or sort the input.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the used-array in the permutations walk.
choices: A start index would generate the same set of permutations just as well.
Once an element's used flag is set, you never need to clear it.
The used-array forces every permutation to be built in increasing order.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A start index would only yield combinations, the flag must be cleared on un-choose to free the element, and permutations are not restricted to increasing order, so none of the listed statements is true.

questions.question: Select all that are true about pruning in backtracking.
choices: Pruning tests a constraint on a partial state before recursing and skips a doomed branch.
An early rejection deletes an entire subtree instead of walking it down to the leaves.
In plain permutations with no constraints, every branch is still pruned before its leaf.
Pruning removes duplicate answers only after the whole search finishes.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Pruning rejects a partial state early and erases the whole doomed subtree. Plain permutations prune nothing because every full path is valid, and pruning is not an end-of-search deduplication step.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the prune, if candidate is greater than remaining then break, in a sorted combination sum.
choices: Because the candidates are sorted, once one overshoots the remaining amount every later candidate overshoots too, so the loop can stop.
The break is still safe even when the candidates are left unsorted.
Sorting is needed here only to remove duplicate candidates.
None of the above
questions.explanation: With sorted candidates the first overshoot guarantees all larger ones overshoot, so break is safe. It depends on the sort, and the sort provides the ordering rather than deduplication.

questions.question: Select all that are true about which problem phrasings point to a backtracking solution.
choices: Return all subsets or combinations of the input.
Produce every valid arrangement or permutation.
Enumerate every configuration that satisfies the constraints.
Return the single shortest path length in an unweighted grid.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Enumerating subsets, arrangements, or every valid configuration are backtracking fingerprints. A single shortest path length is a BFS or dynamic programming job.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about when to prefer dynamic programming over backtracking.
choices: Backtracking is preferred whenever you need one optimum or a count and the subproblems overlap.
Dynamic programming folds an overlapping-subproblem tree into a polynomial table.
You should switch to dynamic programming only when the input is already sorted.
Backtracking has no memory, so it re-walks an exponential or factorial tree.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Statements 0 and 2 are false, so they are the NOT-true selections. Dynamic programming, not backtracking, is the tool when you need one optimum or a count with overlapping subproblems, and the trigger is overlap rather than a sorted input. The other two truly describe dynamic programming folding the tree and backtracking having no memory.
